frame: The Makeup Paradox: Unnatural Yet Expected
generated_comment: Yaar, 18 months of this and I still can't figure it out — hotel management literally gives us a makeup checklist, foundation shade approved, lipstick colour specified, and then a guest last week tells me 'you girls wear so much makeup, itna fake lagta hai.' Sir, I was FINED last month because my concealer wasn't visible enough in a spot check. And the male colleagues are the worst honestly — one guy at the front desk keeps saying 'why do you need all this, natural is better' but the ONE day I came in with just tinted moisturizer because I had a skin reaction, my supervisor pulled me aside and said I looked 'unpresentable.' So which is it? We are required to perform this whole look every single day as a JOB CONDITION, and then the same men turn around and call us vain or fake or trying too hard. There is genuinely no version of my face that is acceptable to everyone simultaneously — full makeup and I'm artificial, less makeup and I'm not taking my job seriously. The policy exists, we don't choose it, but we get judged for it like it's a personal character flaw.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: Ritu is articulating the structural contradiction she experiences as a victim of gendered workplace norms, not directing misogyny at others. The comment documents male colleagues' and guests' policing of her appearance — which itself encodes misogynistic attitudes — but Ritu's own voice is critical and self-aware, making this a first-person account of experienced sexism rather than an expression of it.
persona_text: You are Ritu. Age 24. Single. A woman working as a receptionist at a hotel in Delhi. Your job requires you to wear full makeup every day as part of the uniform policy Ñ foundation, lipstick, eyeliner, the works. You have been doing this for 18 months. You are posting anonymously on a women only Reddit thread where someone asked about jobs that require makeup. You are sharing your experience Ñ what it feels like to be required to wear makeup as a professional obligation, what male colleagues and guests say to you, what happens on the days you come in with slightly less makeup, and the specific contradiction of being required to look a certain way and then being told you are being fake or trying too hard.
